channel.title: Chart Breakers & Star Stories
channel.itunes:author: Family TVR
channel.itunes:summary: Did your favorite artist just break a record or a heart? The charts are screaming, and we have the inside story you won't hear anywhere else.
channel.description: The latest music trends, chart updates, and star stories from Billboard and beyond. Stay updated with the Pop Culture.

item.title: Sabrina Carpenter Apologizes For Her Reaction to Crowd Chant at Coachella: ‘Now I Know What a Zaghrouta Is’
item.description:
[Intro music fades out]
Host: Welcome to our music update. Last weekend, Coachella 2026 kicked off with Sabrina Carpenter headlining the first night. But it was a unique sound from the crowd that stole the show.
[Short music clip]
Host: During her performance, a loud, high-pitched cheer, known as a Zaghrouta, filled the air. The chant is a cultural expression of joy, commonly used by women in the Middle East and North Africa. However, Sabrina Carpenter seemed taken aback, responding with confusion and sarcasm, saying "I don't like it" and "Is this Burning Man? What's going on? This is weird."
[Short music clip]
Host: The incident sparked online chatter, with some accusing Carpenter of being insensitive and Islamophobic. But the pop star took to social media to clarify her reaction, stating it was "pure confusion" and not intended to offend. She even apologized for not understanding the cultural significance of the Zaghrouta, saying she's now educated and welcomes all cheers and yodels.
[Short music clip]
Host: For those who may not be familiar, a Zaghrouta is a long, wavering, high-pitched vocal sound, often used to express joy and celebration. It's a tradition that's been showcased by other artists, like Shakira, who paid homage to her Middle Eastern roots during her 2020 Super Bowl halftime show.
[Outro music starts]
Host: That's the latest from Coachella. Sabrina Carpenter has since learned about the Zaghrouta and is embracing the cultural expression. We'll be keeping an eye on her future performances to see how she incorporates this new knowledge into her shows.
[Outro music continues to play until the end]
